tables.title: Reversible Causes of Cardiac Arrest — The H's and T's (Pediatric-Specific)
rows: Most common in trauma, dehydration from gastroenteritis, hemorrhage; assess for poor turgor, sunken fontanelle in infants
Most common overall cause of pediatric arrest; from airway obstruction, severe pneumonia, status asthmaticus, drowning
Severe metabolic or respiratory acidosis from prolonged arrest, sepsis, or ketoacidosis
Common in infants, neonates, and diabetic children; check glucose early and treat with dextrose
Hypo: digoxin toxicity, renal tubular acidosis. Hyper: renal failure, rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis syndrome
Immersion in cold water, prolonged environmental exposure; may be protective — consider ECPR for severe hypothermia
Clinical diagnosis in arrest — absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, distended neck veins; treat with needle decompression without imaging
From trauma, myocarditis, or pericardial effusion; treat with pericardiocentesis or thoracotomy
Drug ingestion, medication errors, envenomation; consider specific antidotes when the toxin is identified
Massive pulmonary embolism; consider thrombolytics or embolectomy in appropriate patients
Myocardial infarction from anomalous coronary arteries, Kawasaki disease, or thrombosis; rare in children but important to consider

tables.title: Waveform Capnography During CPR — Interpretation Guide
rows: Improve rate, depth, and recoil; rotate compressors; verify technique
Stop compressions; check for pulse; initiate post-arrest care if pulse confirmed

tables.title: Shockable vs Non-Shockable Arrest — Key Differences
rows: Chaotic (VF) or organized wide-complex (pVT) without pulse
Flat line (asystole) or organized rhythm without pulse (PEA)
NOT indicated — does not terminate these rhythms
After second shock or when IV/IO available
ASAP — within 3–5 minutes of arrest onset
Lower overall; depends on identifying and treating reversible causes
